frameworks.name: deep visual encoders
frameworks.one_line: feature embeddings from state-of-the-art deep networks as memory representations

frameworks.name: quantitative VWM recall models
frameworks.one_line: scaling continuous-report recall models from artificial features to natural images

frameworks.name: generative image models
frameworks.one_line: image generation algorithms produce continuously varying response alternatives

core_question: Can a single model of visual working memory recall account for memory errors in both natural images and artificial feature displays?
key_assumptions: trial-by-trial recall errors reflect the geometry of stored representations
continuous report can be extended from simple features to natural images via image-generation
deep encoder features approximate human memory representations
manipulations: stimulus domain: natural images vs artificial feature displays
candidate representation: deep encoder features vs baseline feature spaces
set size in the artificial domain
key_findings: deep encoder features capture coarse-grained and some fine-grained trial-by-trial difficulty in natural images
reasonable baseline representations fail to capture this difficulty structure
deep encoders reproduce set-size effects in the artificial domain
deep encoders provide a poorer overall fit to human recall in artificial displays than in natural images
interpretations: natural and artificial VWM tasks may recruit distinct cognitive systems or brain areas
scaling VWM models to natural images requires representations beyond simple-feature accounts
